video_title: Is Saylor About to Break Bitcoin?
gist: A Bankless rollup dissecting the reflexive risk in crypto treasury companies (DATs): Saylor's small Bitcoin sale signaled weakness and accelerated a selloff, MSTR's Stretch preferred trades 5% below par creating a downward feedback loop, while Bitmine/Tom Lee launch a 9.5% ETH-yield instrument. Hosts debate whether Saylor can ever sell again, whether these yield instruments are Ponzis, why revenue-generating tokens are decoupling from BTC, and why onshore US perps will likely underwhelm versus offshore venues.

themes.theme: Saylor/MSTR reflexivity and the no-sell trap

insights.claim: Strategy (MSTR) sold only 32 BTC for ~$2.5M, but the disclosure of the sale on Tuesday accelerated a Bitcoin selloff from ~$72,000 down to ~$62,000 (~17%), demonstrating the sale's signaling effect far outweighed its tiny size.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: Any future Saylor BTC sale, however small, is now a market-moving negative signal — watch MSTR disclosures as a sentiment catalyst.
insights.quote: Sailor and strategy sold 32 Bitcoin for $2.5 million. A very small sale.

insights.claim: The market has effectively forbidden Saylor from selling Bitcoin: because he is 'Mr. Bitcoin' and a core reason BTC keeps rising, any sale breaks the narrative, so he has likely internalized never to sell again regardless of distress.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: MSTR's optionality to delever via BTC sales is functionally gone — distress will be resolved by equity dilution or skipping preferred dividends, not selling, capping the downside-protection thesis.
insights.quote: Mr. Bitcoin doesn't get to sell. So, if you want to be Mr. Bitcoin, like either one, you got to throw the stretch holders under the bus

insights.claim: Saylor faces a zero-sum trilemma where one stakeholder must lose: he can skip/defer Stretch preferred dividends (screws preferred holders), dilute common via ATM issuance (screws common holders), or sell Bitcoin (breaks the whole system) — the third option is off the table.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: MSTR preferred and common holders are structurally misaligned; in distress, watch whether MSTR defers dividends or hits the ATM to gauge which class gets sacrificed.
insights.quote: one way or another, one of them has to lose because the third category of Bitcoin gets screwed does not work.

insights.claim: Saylor lost far more in NAV from the Bitcoin price drop triggered by his 32-BTC sale than he could have raised in cash, proving that funding obligations via BTC sales is net value-destructive for MSTR.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Confirms BTC sales are a self-defeating funding source for large DATs — reinforces dilution as the only viable lever.
insights.quote: He lost so much more money in his nav because of how much Bitcoin went down from that sale relative to the amount of cash you would need to raise

insights.claim: The contrarian alternative view is that Saylor should take a single large one-time BTC sale of ~$500-600M to fund Stretch dividends for 3+ years and restore confidence, then never sell again — the 'least bad' way if he must sell at all.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: If MSTR ever does a single large pre-funding BTC sale, it could be read as confidence-restoring rather than capitulation — a non-consensus interpretation to watch.
insights.quote: I think Sailor just needs to take it on the chin, make a large single cell of Bitcoin, and that's it. And then he just clears it.

insights.claim: MSTR now holds such a large share of the Bitcoin market that it has become 'the market' — the DAT endgame — so if it voluntarily capitulates, other holders front-run by selling, unlike its smaller 2022 BTC sale which markets absorbed.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Position-size reflexivity means the largest BTC treasuries lose the ability to transact without moving price against themselves — a structural fragility as DATs scale.
insights.quote: this is kind of the endgame for DATs is like once you become big enough you are the market.

themes.theme: Stretch preferred as a confidence canary

insights.claim: MSTR's Stretch preferred trades at ~$95.3, about 5% below its $100 peg, with a market cap of ~$9.9B versus a ~$10.5B par — roughly $561M below par — signaling eroding market confidence.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: Stretch's discount to par is a real-time confidence gauge for the MSTR financing machine — watch it as a leading indicator of forced action.
insights.quote: Stretch is trading at $95 $95.3. That's about 5% off of its peg... We're we're $561 million below par.

insights.claim: Stretch below par creates a self-reinforcing downward spiral: traders anticipate Saylor must sell Bitcoin to fund the dividend and close the gap, so they front-run by selling their own BTC, pushing price down further.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: The Stretch discount and BTC price are reflexively linked — a widening discount can mechanically pressure spot BTC via front-running flows.
insights.quote: I'm going to get out of the way and I'm going to sell my Bitcoin and so it's is creating a downward a downwards feedback downward spiral.

insights.claim: Stretch dividends run over $1 billion per year while MSTR has only ~$700M cash on hand, meaning the company cannot self-fund the preferred dividend from cash and must dilute or defer.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: The dividend-vs-cash gap quantifies MSTR's funding squeeze; deferral or ATM dilution becomes near-inevitable in a prolonged downturn.
insights.quote: the stretch dividends are like over a billion a year and he has 700 million on hand

insights.claim: Stretch's structure gives MSTR discretion to skip/defer preferred dividends (they accrue and eventually pay), giving Saylor a release valve to avoid selling Bitcoin in distress.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Stretch holders bear deferral risk; the favorable-to-issuer terms mean preferred holders, not BTC, absorb stress first.
insights.quote: you can miss a dividend and and you know, they have the discretion to do that.

themes.theme: Bitmine/Tom Lee ETH-yield instrument vs MSTR model

insights.claim: Bitmine (Tom Lee) filed for a Stretch-style preferred stock offering yielding 9.5%, launched amid market weakness, signaling intent to accelerate ETH accumulation rather than slow down.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: Bitmine is replicating the DAT preferred-yield playbook on ETH — a 9.5% coupon is the new benchmark to compare against Stretch and gauge ETH-DAT financing demand.
insights.quote: Bitmine has filed for a preferred stock offering offering a yield of 9.5%.

insights.claim: Bitmine is structurally advantaged over MSTR because ETH has a native staking yield, so it can fund the 9.5% preferred coupon by selling staking yield rather than selling principal ETH — meaning no net new ETH sales required.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: ETH-based DATs have a narratively cleaner funding model than BTC-based ones; favors Bitmine's structure over MSTR's on a financing-resilience basis.
insights.quote: because Ether has a yield, you can actually pay the yield uh here by just selling the yield and not selling the the principal.

insights.claim: Funding ETH-DAT yield from staking is economically the same as selling principal — it is ETH inflation moving from one pocket to another, with someone still needing cash to buy the sold ETH; the difference is narrative, not economics.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Don't be fooled by the 'still accumulating' framing — ETH staking-funded yield still generates real sell pressure; the bull case rests on narrative-driven flows, not superior economics.
insights.quote: I think that is a narrative transformation, not an actual economic transformation, per se, because of course that's, you know, inflation to the total e supply.

insights.claim: Whether ETH staking yield is 'real' depends on the EIP-1559 burn: with no burn the staking yield is just issuance (nominal), but if L1 burn rises the yield becomes real — making increased ETH burn a precondition for the bull case on Bitmine's instrument.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: Watch ETH net issuance/burn dynamics as the variable that converts Bitmine's yield instrument from a dilution shell game into a real-yield product.
insights.quote: with the burn that that yield goes from nominal to real. And that is the strength here that I see

insights.claim: Tom Lee/Bitmine has a potential second-mover advantage by learning from Saylor's mistake of aggressively over-issuing Stretch, whose negative consequences are now surfacing.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: Later-entrant ETH DATs may price/size their preferred issuance more conservatively, reducing reflexive blowup risk relative to MSTR.
insights.quote: Tom Lee can just learn from Sailor's mistakes. I think Sailor... really just issued the crap out of Stretch aggressively

insights.claim: Issuing a yield instrument is inconsistent with Bitmine's stated plan to cap ETH holdings at 5% of supply and slow purchases near 4.5% — a yield offering is a 'speed-up' move, suggesting Tom Lee will likely abandon the 5% cap as he approaches it.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Expect Bitmine to raise or scrap its 5% ETH-supply target; a removed cap reignites the accumulation narrative and the stock's premium.
insights.quote: this um you know this offering doesn't make a lot of sense if you're slowing down, right? This is a speed up kind of offering.

insights.claim: Tom Lee is personally incentivized to grow Bitmine as large as possible because his compensation scales with the entity's size, making the 5% self-imposed cap unlikely to hold.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Manager incentives point toward maximal ETH accumulation — a structural driver of persistent ETH bid from Bitmine.
insights.quote: Tom Lee gets paid the bigger this this thing becomes. It becomes really big he makes more money.

themes.theme: ETH concentration and Ethereum consensus risk

insights.claim: Unlike Bitcoin (proof-of-work, ownership-neutral), owning large ETH and staking it confers governance/consensus power; ~40% of ETH is staked, so an entity needs ~33% of staked ETH (~13% of total supply) to threaten consensus.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: ETH DATs have a hard ceiling that BTC DATs lack — at ~13% of supply an ETH treasury becomes a consensus-security risk, capping how large Bitmine can safely grow.
insights.quote: you have to get to 33% of total state teeth in order to basically be able to cause a a major failure in consensus. And so that takes you to what is that 13%.

insights.claim: At 4.5% of ETH supply (~$10B), Bitmine is only about 2.5x away from the ~13% consensus-threat threshold (~$25B at current prices), meaning there is little headroom before it would break Ethereum's security model.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Bitmine's runway for accumulation is far shorter than MSTR's effectively unlimited BTC runway — a structural growth cap unique to ETH DATs.
insights.quote: if uh if Tom Lee goes to 13%, that's like a two and a half times where he currently is. Tom Lee is about 10 billion.

insights.claim: A public-company ETH steward subpoenaed to enforce OFAC compliance could pressure Ethereum's credible neutrality (e.g., refusing to validate non-compliant transactions), creating a governance-capture risk if Bitmine controls a third of staked ETH.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: A regulated megaholder introduces censorship/neutrality risk to Ethereum — a tail risk for ETH's value narrative if DAT concentration continues.
insights.quote: if you have one public company that is the major, like the largest steward of Ethereum and has the ability to kind of stop the show if they want to.

insights.claim: If Bitmine grows too large it could mitigate consensus-capture concerns by routing ETH into DeFi instead of staking, delegating to third-party stakers like Lido, or pledging ETH into blind trusts with independent governance.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Watch for Bitmine governance-distancing structures (blind trusts, third-party staking) as a tell that it is approaching consensus-relevant scale.
insights.quote: maybe even we put it into blind trusts and those trusts are responsible for staking

insights.claim: Bitmine is in pole position to become the institutional economic advocate for ETH-the-asset, filling the vacuum left by the Ethereum Foundation's intentional step-back and EF brain-drain.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: A financially-aligned megaholder advocating for ETH financialization and market competitiveness could be a structural ETH bull catalyst, offsetting EF passivity.
insights.quote: I seere increasingly Bitmine being positioned to take that torch.

themes.theme: DAT yield instruments: Ponzi or not / leverage framing

insights.claim: DAT yield instruments are not Ponzis because a Ponzi is guaranteed to fail, whereas these are risky leveraged bets whose survival depends on volatility and the asset's demand floor — small leverage on BTC bought at $5K may never get margin-called.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Reframe DATs as variable-leverage trusts, not frauds; assess each by leverage ratio and liquidation level relative to the asset's historical floor.
insights.quote: A Ponzi is guaranteed to fail... These are clearly not guaranteed to fail. They might fail, but they're not guaranteed to fail.

insights.claim: Bitmine currently has no debt and no leverage structure on its cap table, so as-is it is functionally a trust (like GBTC) and cannot be a Ponzi; the proposed preferred would be its first structural leverage.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Bitmine's clean balance sheet means current downside is pure NAV exposure; the preferred offering is the inflection point where leverage risk begins.
insights.quote: Bitmine has no structure. There's no debt. There's no anything... clearly asis Bitmine is not a Ponzi.

insights.claim: DATs are leveraged 'number-go-up' bets on very favorable terms — the leverage is small relative to the balance sheet, but it only pays if the underlying rises; if number goes down, they lose.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: DAT equity is a levered directional bet — size exposure accordingly and don't mistake the favorable financing terms for downside protection.
insights.quote: getting levered only works when number go up.

insights.claim: There is a 'goldilocks zone' of leverage where part of the market doubts the DAT will survive but it doesn't fail; if confidence is never tested (Stretch never trades off peg), the DAT is taking too little risk and leaving returns on the table.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: Periodic confidence scares in MSTR/Stretch may be a feature of optimal leverage, not necessarily a sign of impending failure — a contrarian lens on volatility.
insights.quote: if you never have these moments where part of the market like shrugs off... then actually you're not taking enough risk

themes.theme: ETF outflows and flow-driven market structure

insights.claim: Bitcoin spot ETFs are on a record 13-day consecutive outflow streak (per Galaxy Research), one of the most bearish stretches in BTC ETF history, with ETH ETFs likely matching.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: Record ETF outflows confirm sustained institutional/retail de-risking — a flow headwind until the streak breaks.
insights.quote: Bitcoin spot ETF outflows are on a record setting 13-day streak of consecutive out outflows.

insights.claim: ETF flow data is backward-looking and tautological — outflows merely memorialize selling that already happened and cannot explain why people sold, so they should not be treated as a forward signal.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Stop reading ETF outflows as predictive; they are a record of past price action, not a leading indicator — avoid the circular-reasoning trap.
insights.quote: No this memorializes the selling. This is a record of the selling. Asking why did they sell like this this this chart cannot answer that for you.

insights.claim: The current crypto market is heavily flows- and sentiment-driven rather than fundamentals-driven, with Bitcoin about to cross below its 200-day moving average after a 12-13% weekly drop.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: In a flow/sentiment regime, technical levels (200DMA) and narrative matter more than valuation — trade reflexively, not fundamentally.
insights.quote: I think it's pretty clear this market is very flows driven right now and very sentiment driven.

insights.claim: ETFs are not a one-way 'up only' ratchet — they sell as readily as they buy — whereas DATs are the true permanent bid, which is why DAT narrative (perpetual buyer) drives flows.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: DATs, not ETFs, provide the structural perpetual bid for BTC/ETH — but only as long as the 'always buying' narrative holds.
insights.quote: the ETFs are not up only. They are not a one-way ratchet... these DATs are that.

insights.claim: Tom Lee/Bitmine is down ~$8.9B on its $10B ETH position and Saylor is down ~$7.6B on $56B of Bitcoin, quantifying the unrealized losses at the major DATs during the drawdown.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: Sizeable unrealized losses raise the stakes on DAT funding decisions; Bitmine's loss being near its full position size highlights its concentration.
insights.quote: Tom Lee is down $ 8.9 billion on 10 billion of ETH... Michael Taylor is down $7.6 billion. He has 56 billion of Bitcoin.

themes.theme: Token decoupling: revenue-driven altcoins resist BTC drawdown

insights.claim: For the first time across recent cycles, several legitimate tokens hit all-time highs and showed strength in the same week Bitcoin fell to its 200-day MA and ETH sat ~30% below its 200DMA — a structural break from prior 'everything dumps together' behavior.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: Crypto is bifurcating into BTC-correlated and revenue-decoupled assets — alpha is increasingly in cash-flow tokens that don't need a BTC rally.
insights.quote: Never before have I seen tokens reaching all-time highs and having strength in that same week.

insights.claim: Hyperliquid is decoupling from Bitcoin because a growing share of its volume comes from Trade XYZ trading RWAs rather than crypto perps, so its revenue narrative is less tied to BTC price.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Hyperliquid's RWA-trading mix is its decoupling driver — track RWA volume share as the fundamental that justifies its premium independent of BTC.
insights.quote: more and more of their volume is actually coming from Trade XYZ, which is trading RWAS.

insights.claim: Venice's token price moves with demand for AI tokens (inference) on its platform rather than crypto prices, making it materially diversified out of the crypto market.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Venice is effectively an AI-demand proxy, not a crypto-beta asset — useful for exposure to AI usage without BTC correlation.
insights.quote: As demand for AI tokens goes up, Venice's revenues increase and Venice goes up.

insights.claim: AI human-identity tokens led the week's gainers: 'humanity' up 120%, Worldcoin up 94%, with Worldcoin's rally attributed to OpenAI IPO anticipation (alongside Anthropic and SpaceX 'infinity trade' speculation).
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: OpenAI/IPO-linked narratives are driving identity tokens; watch Worldcoin as a high-beta proxy for AI-IPO sentiment, with memecoin-like risk.
insights.quote: humanity is up 120%, Worldcoin is up 94% this week... OpenAI IPO anticipation. That seems to be what people are claiming is causing this.

insights.claim: Other week-gainers cited: Lighter +30%, Ethena (ENA) +11%, Hyperliquid +10%, Venice +9%, Jupiter +6%, while majors XRP and Solana mirrored BTC/ETH weakness.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: A concrete watchlist of relative-strength tokens during a BTC drawdown — candidates for the revenue-decoupling thesis.
insights.quote: we got Lighter up 30%, Athena up 11%, Hyperlid up 10%, Venice up 9%, Jupiter up 6%.

insights.claim: The decoupling cuts both ways: if revenue-driven tokens like Hyperliquid are priced on revenues (much of it RWA, not crypto-correlated), they may not double just because BTC rises — they could capture less crypto-beta on the upside too.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Revenue-decoupled tokens may underperform in a BTC melt-up — don't assume current relative strength implies amplified upside when crypto-beta returns.
insights.quote: it may well be that actually people are pricing hyperlquid on revenues... they just don't capture as much cryptobate on the way up either.

insights.claim: Revenue does not universally protect tokens: most DeFi (non-perp) is getting hammered as multiples compress and crypto-denominated revenues are expected to fall, and Near sold off hard the same day despite being revenue-driven.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: The 'revenue saves you' thesis is selective — perp/RWA-exposed names hold up while crypto-revenue DeFi gets de-rated; differentiate by revenue denomination.
insights.quote: DeFi is getting hammered if it's not a perpex right so it's it's not true across the board that okay well revenue will save you.

insights.claim: Zcash is the one token in the host's focus list that is not revenue-driven and instead trades as a 'metaphorically paired' Bitcoin proxy due to its 21M fixed supply, explaining its larger pullback.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: Zcash behaves as BTC-beta rather than a cash-flow asset — group it with majors, not with revenue-decoupled tokens.
insights.quote: that is the one that is not revenue driven. that is more of just like medically paired with Bitcoin because it's the same same thing 21 million units.

themes.theme: US onshore perps: regulatory greenlight, structural skepticism

insights.claim: The CFTC licensed Kalshi for the first US Bitcoin perpetual futures contract and issued a no-action letter to Coinbase to connect customers as 'the pipes' to a perp platform, signaling the opening of onshore US perps.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: Onshore perps are now regulator-sanctioned — a multi-year structural story for venues (Kalshi, Coinbase, Polymarket, Robinhood, Lighter) competing for US perp flow.
insights.quote: it uh gave a license to Khi for the first Bitcoin perpetual futures contract inside United States.

insights.claim: Onshore US perps will likely underwhelm because retail isn't actively here, US regulation caps leverage well below offshore (likely under 20x vs 50x), and much volume will be displacement from spot rather than net-new demand.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Fade the 'US perps = altcoin renaissance' hype; expect muted near-term impact and continued offshore volume dominance.
insights.quote: the idea that launching domestic per is going to result in this renaissance of demand for altcoins or for crypto um I I think is a bit of a fantasy.

insights.claim: The host predicts the Bitcoin price will barely move when US perps go live; if BTC instead surges on incremental US demand, that will be the falsification of the skeptical thesis.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: A clean, falsifiable trade signal: watch BTC reaction at US perp launch to confirm/deny the muted-impact thesis.
insights.quote: I would predict that you don't actually see the Bitcoin price move very much once these perks launch domestically.

insights.claim: The CFTC explicitly says perps are appropriate for crypto but not for equities or agricultural products, and won't allow willy-nilly RWA perps because incumbents (CME, CBOE) and the ag lobby have powerful vested interests in going first.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Equity/RWA perps face incumbent capture — expect CME/CBOE, not crypto startups, to lead any 24/7 equity-perp expansion; bet on incumbents.
insights.quote: CFTC has explicitly said that they are not encouraging people to apply with PERPS for equity instruments or for securities.

insights.claim: Perps thrived specifically because crypto is global, unregulated, and lacks credit/clawback mechanisms; in the US traditional system where debts can be sent to collections, the structural need for perps is weaker — explaining why perps never spread to other markets in 10+ years.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Perps may be inherently an offshore instrument; their onshore adoption is structurally limited, supporting the muted-impact view.
insights.quote: part of why pers thrived is in this environment of being offshore and not having the ability to you know basically claw back money from people who are deeply read

insights.claim: The bull path for US perps is gradual marginal adoption that, by ~2028, brings a novel simple slider/leverage UX to Coinbase early in the next cycle, going viral and helping fuel a euphoric blowoff top.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: If perps matter, it's a late-2027/2028 cycle catalyst via retail UX virality, not an immediate 2026 demand driver.
insights.quote: in like 2028 people think Bitcoin is cool again... there's this sick slider mechanism that they've never seen before... that's how you get the blowoff top

insights.claim: Coinbase bought ENA (Ethena's token) on the open market and flagged a partnership to embed an Ethena delta-neutral yield product (4-7% from perp funding) into its front end, replicating the 'DeFi mullet' model it used with Morpho's BTC-backed loans.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: Coinbase is productizing DeFi yield via front-end integrations — bullish for ENA/Ethena distribution and the 'DeFi mullet' (CeFi front, DeFi back) model.
insights.quote: Coinbase bought ENA, that's the token of Athena on the open market this week and also flagged a new partnership.

insights.claim: The Coinbase-Ethena yield product may be a creative workaround to the Clarity Act's restriction on stablecoin issuers paying yield: if a third party (Ethena) generates a net-new yield source rather than passing through Treasury yield, it can be shared with users economically equivalent to stablecoin yield.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Regulatory arbitrage around the Clarity Act yield ban could become a template for CeFi platforms to deliver yield — a structural tailwind for DeFi-yield integrations.
insights.quote: Clarity um kind of revises the Genius Act by restricting your ability to pay yield... if it's not you paying the yield... technically that allows them to get yield

insights.claim: Ethena has evolved away from relying almost entirely on the basis/carry trade for yield, because the carry has weakened as crypto basis compressed in a bearish market, diversifying its yield sources.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Ethena's yield resilience now depends on diversification beyond basis trade — monitor its yield mix as basis stays compressed in bear conditions.
insights.quote: it used to be that almost all their yield came from the the the carry trade or the basis trade. Um and that has evolved as the carry trade has really kind of weakened

insights.claim: The Clarity/Genius Act yield restrictions apply only to Americans, so Coinbase can still pay stablecoin yield to its large non-American user base, implying possible region-differentiated versions of the Ethena product.
insights.speaker: guest (Hib)
insights.so_what: Geographic yield asymmetry means non-US users remain a growth lever for yield products — relevant for Coinbase's international monetization.
insights.quote: the Genius Act or Android the Clarity revision to Genius only applies to Americans and Coinbase has a lot of non-American users.
